segments.s393-g97: نهادند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of نهادن (to place/lay), used here in a literary register. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; گفتند is the past 3rd person plural of گفتن (they said) and governs the following prepositional phrase. با + کردگار is a prepositional phrase: با normally means 'with' but here marks the addressee/presence ('to/with the Creator') and کردگار is the divine noun 'Creator/God'.
segments.s393-g98: توی here is an archaic/poetic form functioning as the copula 'thou art' (equivalent to تویی/توئی), introducing the predicate. پاک and بی‌عیب are predicative adjectives 'pure' and 'faultless' joined by و 'and', and پروردگار ('Lord'/'Nurturer') is a further nominal predicate/appellative; together they form the content of what is said to the Creator.
segments.s393-g81: پشوتن (proper name) is the subject; بیامد is a simple past verb ‘came’. هم here is an adverbial particle meaning ‘also/too’. اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'in/at the time' (اندر is an older/preclassical preposition meaning 'in/at'). Word order is Subject + Past Verb + adverbials.
segments.s393-g82: به نزدیک is a prepositional unit meaning ‘near/close to’ (به introducing the direction/position). آن is the demonstrative 'that' pointing to a previously known person; نامدار جهان is a two-word epithet 'famed/renowned of the world' (نامدار qualified by جهان). The whole phrase functions as an adverbial/complement indicating whom he approached.
segments.s393-g83: جهانجوی is an epithet/noun 'world-seeker' (the subject). چون introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when/as'; چشمها is the direct object 'his/the eyes' and باز کرد is the compound verbal idea 'opened' (باز کردن), with کرد as the past auxiliary. The clause reads 'when he opened his eyes.'
segments.s393-g84: به introduces the indirect object/patient 'to'; گردان here means 'troop' (a group of fighters) and گردنکش is an adjectival epithet 'headstrong/rebellious' modifying گردان. آواز کردن is an idiom 'to call out' or 'to cry (a call)'; کرد is the past form. The phrase means 'he called out to the headstrong troop.'
segments.s393-g85: که introduces reported speech/content ('that' or the words he spoke). بیهوش گشتم is a past-stative construction meaning 'I became unconscious' (گشتم = became). من is the first-person pronoun 'I' placed after the verb for emphasis/order. از دود زهر is an ablative/cause phrase 'from the poisonous smoke' (از marks cause/source).
segments.s393-g86: ز زخمش 'from his wound' (ز as poetic 'از'); نیامد is past negative 'did not come' (lit. 'did not come to be'); مرا is split in the text as م + را: here it functions as the indirect object 'to me' (م = me; را = object/definiteness marker attached separately in the manuscript split). هیچ بهر = 'any/any kind of help/benefit' (هیچ = none/any, بهر = benefit/for). The clause literally says 'no help came to me from his wound.'
segments.s393-g87: از آن خاک برخاست is a simple past-clause 'he rose from that ground/dust' (از marks source; برخاست = rose up). و شد سوی آب uses شد (a past auxiliary/verb 'became/went') plus سوی 'toward' and آب 'the water' to express movement 'and turned/moved toward the water.'
segments.s393-g88: چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; مردی 'a man' is the head of the simile; که introduces the relative clause 'who ...'; بیهوش گردد uses گردد (a present/subjunctive form of شدن/گشتن) to mean 'becomes/falls' unconscious — subjunctive/present form is common in subordinate similes; به خواب 'into sleep' (به as 'into/toward' here). The whole unit means 'like a man who becomes unconscious into sleep.'
segments.s393-g1: بفرمود is an honorific/simple past verb meaning 'he ordered/said' placed at the start. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that/so that') and links the main verb to what follows. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'before him / in his presence', and گرگسار is a noun (an epithet = 'wolf‑like' or 'ferocious') that serves as the subject of the subordinate clause and will be completed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s393-g2: بیامد is the simple past 'he came' and here completes the clause introduced by تا; the subject (گرگسار) is understood from the previous segment. بداندیش and بد روزگار are predicative descriptions: بداندیش 'ill‑intentioned/malicious' and بد روزگار a compound meaning 'of ill fate / unfortunate', joined by و 'and'.
segments.s393-g3: The phrase begins with the numeral + noun 'سه جام' ('three cups'); می‌لعل modifies جام as an adjective/genitive 'ruby‑hued wine'. فامش = 'its hue' (فام + ـش), and بداد here is a poetic past verb meaning 'he gave' (an archaic/poetic form of داد), so the verb comes after its object phrase, which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s393-g4: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. آهرمن is the proper name 'Ahriman' (the devil). از جام is a causal/ablative phrase 'from the cup' and می‌گشت is an imperfective/past progressive 'was becoming / grew (to be)', so the clause means 'when Ahriman grew merry from the cup'; شاد is the predicate adjective 'merry/happy'.
segments.s393-g5: بدو here is the prepositional/pronominal 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the past tense verb 'he said' (the provided sense list is a noun so the appropriate verbal sense is not listed — see senses). کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by the addressed noun phrase 'مرد بدبخت خوار' = 'wretched, despised man' (two adjectives following the noun in apposition).
segments.s393-g6: که introduces an indirect question/clause here (roughly 'that / what'); فردا is temporal 'tomorrow / the future'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; پیش آورد is a light/verbal phrase 'bring forth / cause to come' (پیش + آورد = 'bring about'); and روزگار is 'fate / the times'. The whole clause asks 'what will tomorrow/fate bring?'.
segments.s393-g7: بدو 'to him' again marks the indirect object. گفت is the past 'he said' (verbal sense not listed). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing شاه 'king'; برتر منش is a descriptive compound 'of superior/exalted character' (برتر modifying منش). The sequence is a direct address following the reporting verb.
segments.s393-g8: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from'; تو = 'you' (second person); دور = 'far / distant'. بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (wishing clause). بد and بدکنش are the wished‑for items: بد = 'evil / ill', and بدکنش = 'the ill‑doer' or 'wrong‑doer'. The clause literally says 'may evil and the evildoer be far from you.'
segments.s393-g41: Subject 'جهان' (the world) comes first, followed by the past verb 'گشت' (became/turned). 'چون' introduces a simile ('like'), and the phrase 'روی زنگی سیاه' is a noun phrase 'the face of a zangi, black' where 'روی' (face) is modified by the adjectives/noun 'زنگی' + 'سیاه' (dark-skinned/black). This is poetic inversion but otherwise a straightforward simile structure.
segments.s393-g42: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'برج حمل' is a genitival compound 'the sign/tower of Ḥamal (Aries)'. The clause ends with 'تاج بنمود ماه' in which 'بنمود' is the past simple 'showed/appeared' and the subject 'ماه' (the moon) follows the verb by poetic inversion: literally 'a crown showed the moon' → 'the moon displayed a crown.'
segments.s393-g43: Here 'نشست' is the past verb 'sat up / rose' (classical usage). The prepositional phrase 'از بر شولک' ('from upon the sholak') follows, and the proper name 'اسفندیار' appears at the end as the subject — a common poetic word order (verb + circumstantial phrase + subject).
segments.s393-g44: 'برفت' here functions as a poetic past of 'رفت' meaning 'proceeded/advanced' (the given lemma senses are not applicable to this verbal use). 'از پسش' means 'behind him/after him' (positional), and 'لشکر نامدار' is the subject/noun phrase 'the renowned army/host' that performs the action. Word order places the verb before its subject, as is common in the epic style.
segments.s393-g45: 'دگر روز' = 'the next/other day' prefaces the temporal clause. 'چون' here functions as a temporal/connective ('when' / 'since'), and 'گشت روشن جهان' is 'the world became bright' — verb 'گشت' (became) with predicate 'روشن' (bright) and subject 'جهان' placed after it in poetic order.
segments.s393-g46: 'درفش شب' is a genitival/attributive compound 'the banner (standard) of night' (poetic juxtaposition rather than explicit izafe). 'تیره شد' is the past construction 'became dark,' and the adverbial 'در نهان' ('in secrecy/hiddenly') modifies the verb. The clause means the night's standard grew/was darkened secretly.
segments.s393-g47: 'پشوتن' is the proper name (subject) and 'بیامد' is the past verb 'came.' 'سوی نامجوی' is the prepositional complement 'toward Namjuy.' Word order is subject + verb + directional phrase, the normal narrative sequence.
segments.s393-g48: 'پسر' is the noun 'son' (subject); 'با برادر' is an accompanying phrase 'with (his) brother.' The particle 'همی' is an emphatic/continuative particle used in epic Persian to mark the action, and 'پیش اوی' means 'in his presence / before him' where 'اوی' is the pronoun 'him.' The clause reads 'the son, with his brother, was before him.'
segments.s393-g49: بپوشید is a past, 3rd‑person singular verb ('he clothed/put on') placed before its objects as is common in poetic Persian. خفتان is an archaic poetic noun here meaning a coat of mail/armour (treated as the direct object). جهاندار identifies the person clothed ('the sovereign, world‑ruler'), and گرد functions adverbially/locatively ('around/on/having been placed on') to indicate the armour being put on him.
segments.s393-g50: سپه را is the definite direct object ('the army' + object marker را). به + فرخ پشوتن is the indirect object phrase 'to/for the fortunate Peshotan' (فرخ = 'fortunate', پشوتن = proper name). سپرد is the past 3rd‑person singular verb 'he entrusted/handed over', completing the clause.
segments.s393-g51: بیاورد is a past 3rd‑person verb 'he brought/produced' placed before its objects in the poetic order. گردون and صندوق شیر are coordinated objects joined by و; صندوق شیر is a genitive construction 'the chest/box of the lion' (noun + modifying noun).
segments.s393-g52: نشست is past 3rd‑person 'sat' and here functions as the main verb. اندر او is a locative prepositional phrase 'inside it' (او refers back to the chest). شهریار دلیر is the subject noun phrase 'the brave king' placed after the verb in poetic inversion.
segments.s393-g53: دو اسپ گرانمایه is a noun phrase 'two prized/precious steeds'. بسته is a past participle/adjunct 'bound/tied' that follows the noun, and بر اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto him' (اوی = 'him'). The whole chunk describes the horses being fastened to him.
segments.s393-g54: سوی اژدها is the directional phrase 'toward the dragon'. تیز is an adverb/adjective 'swiftly/quick', and بنهاد is a past verb meaning 'he placed/set' (here used with تیز to mean 'he quickly turned/put'); روی ('face') is the object placed after the verb in poetic order, so the clause reads 'he swiftly turned his face toward the dragon.'
segments.s393-g55: ز دور is a prepositional expression 'from afar' or 'at a distance'. اژدها بانگ is an inversion for 'the dragon's cry' (noun + noun instead of the genitive order). گردون here is a locative/majestic adverbial 'through the heavens' or 'resounding across the sky', and شنید is the past verb 'he heard.'
segments.s393-g56: خرامیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the striding/prancing'. اسپ جنگی is the noun phrase 'the war‑horse' (اسپ = horse; جنگی = warlike). بدید is the past 3rd‑person verb 'he saw', so the clause literally says 'he saw the war‑horse's striding.'
segments.s393-g73: ز (preposition) + گردون (noun) marks origin: 'from the heavens'. The coordinating و links 'گردون' with 'آن تیغها' ('those swords'). شد غمی is a simple past copular/eventive clause: شد here = 'arose/occurred' and غمی is the predicative noun 'a grief'. Word order is prepositional phrase → subject/agent phrase → verb → predicative noun.
segments.s393-g74: به زور is an adverbial phrase meaning 'by force' (به used here adverbially). اندر آورد is a verb phrase where اندر is the locative/introductory particle and آورد (here sense 'cause, bring about') is the main verb 'brought about/caused'. لختی کمی is an adverbial time phrase 'for a short while' (لختی = 'a short while', کمی = 'a little') modifying the verb.
segments.s393-g75: برآمد is a past verb 'emerged/issued forth'. ز صندوق is the source phrase 'from the chest/box'. مرد دلیر is the nominative/subject 'a brave man' that follows the verb (typical epic inversion: verb before subject).
segments.s393-g76: This is an appositive description of the emerging figure: یکی = 'a certain/one', تیز شمشیر = 'a sharp sword'. در چنگ شیر is a prepositional phrase 'in the claw/grip of the lion' (در = 'in', چنگ = 'claw/grip', شیر = 'lion'), giving a vivid image of how the sword is held.
segments.s393-g77: به شمشیر uses به in an instrumental/adverbial sense 'with/by the sword'. مغزش is the possessed noun 'his brain' (مغز + possessive -ش). همی is an archaic continuous/progressive marker ('was/then'), and کرد چاک is a light-verb construction where کرد('made/did') + چاک('a gash/split') = 'made a gash' (i.e., 'he gashed/split').
segments.s393-g78: همی again gives a continuous/iterative tone. دود زهرش is a genitive phrase 'the smoke of his poison' (زهر + possessive -ش). برآمد ز خاک is the verb phrase 'rose up from the earth' (برآمد = 'arose/issued forth', ز خاک = 'from the ground/earth').
segments.s393-g79: از آن دود marks source 'from that smoke'. برنده here is an adjective meaning 'biting/pungent' modifying دود. بیهوش گشت is a past-result clause: بیهوش = 'unconscious' and گشت = 'became/turned', so 'became unconscious'.
segments.s393-g80: بیفتاد = 'he fell' (simple past). The following coordinated predicates و بی‌مغز و بی‌توش گشت use بی- compounds: بی‌مغز 'brainless' and بی‌توش (an idiomatic adjective) 'without tosh/obscure/stripped of something' and گشت 'became' completes the clause meaning 'and he fell and became brainless and became without tosh.'
segments.s393-g9: چو here means 'like/as' (a simile), not temporal 'when'; آتش is the image 'fire' (the likened thing). به + پیکار = 'into/onto battle' (به = towards/into). بشتافتی is the 2nd-person singular past (classical) form 'you hastened/you rushed' (subject omitted in the line, understood as 'you'). The clause is a simile: 'you rushed into battle like fire.'
segments.s393-g10: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' linking result to the previous simile. بر here carries the sense 'over/against' (indicating dealing with or passing over something). بلاها is the plural 'calamities/afflictions'. گذر is a noun 'passage/way' and یافتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you found' (used here to mean 'you would find/obtain' — a consequence of the previous clause).
segments.s393-g11: ندانی is 2nd-person singular present negative 'you do not know' (classical present). که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that/what'. فردا = 'tomorrow' (future time). چه آیدت = 'what comes to you / what will befall you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you' or 'befalls you'); پیش here functions adverbially as 'ahead/in store' (lit. 'before'). Together: 'you do not know what tomorrow will bring you.'
segments.s393-g12: ببخشای is an archaic imperative/hortative 2nd‑person form meaning 'spare/forgive/grant' (here a piece of advice: give or spare something). بر بخت بیدار خویش is a prepositional/nominal phrase: بر = 'upon/for', بخت بیدار = 'wakeful/alert fortune' (bīdār as adjective), and خوی + ش together form خویش 'one's own' (the tokenization shows stem + possessive). The whole clause: 'spare it for your own awakened fate/fortune.'
segments.s393-g13: از ایدر (از = 'from', ایدر = archaic 'here') starts the temporal/locative setting. چو = 'when' (temporal), فردا = 'tomorrow', به منزل رسی = 'you arrive at the dwelling/stage' (به = to/towards, منزل = dwelling/station/stop, رسی = 2nd‑person form 'you reach/arrive'). The clause sets the time/place: 'when tomorrow you arrive here at the dwelling.'
segments.s393-g14: یکی کار پیش است = 'one matter/affair lies before/awaits (you)' (یکی = 'one/a certain', کار = 'affair', پیش = 'before/ahead', است = 'is'). از این یک بسی = 'from this, one suffices' or 'this one is enough' (از = 'from/of', این = 'this', یک بسی = 'one is enough' — بسی = 'enough/to suffice'). The two clauses together: one business awaits you, and that single business is sufficient.
segments.s393-g15: یکی = 'one/a certain' introducing an indefinite subject; اژدها = 'dragon/monster' (subject). پیشت = 'before you / in front of you' (prepositional adverb). آید is the present/future 3rd‑person verb 'comes/will come'. دژم is an adjective 'grim/dreadful' placed after the verb as a predicate/adjective describing the dragon (poetic word order).
segments.s393-g16: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. ماهی = 'fish' (object). برآرد is a classical 3rd‑person singular present/future 'brings forth/raises up'. ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/out of', دریا = 'sea', and به + دم here indicates the manner/means — دم literally 'breath/moment/tip' in the listed senses; in context it likely means 'by its tail' or 'with a single motion/beat' (a compact poetic image). So: 'that (dragon) brings fish out of the sea with a (tail-)motion.'
segments.s393-g25: ببینی is an optative/predictive second‑person singular form (from دیدن) used like 'you will see' or 'see that'. که introduces the content clause that follows. از چنگ من is a prepositional phrase 'from my grip' (از = from; چنگ = 'claw/grip') and اژدها is the noun 'the dragon' placed after that phrase as the clause's topic/object.
segments.s393-g26: ز is the poetic variant of از ('from/by'). شمشیر تیزم = شمشیر + adjective تیز + possessive suffix -م 'my', so 'my sharp sword'. نیابد is the third‑person singular negative present of یابیدن ('does not/ will not find'); here it governs the following noun رها ('release, freedom') — together نیابد رها = 'will not find release' / 'will not be freed'.
segments.s393-g27: بفرمود is an honorific/past third‑person verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('that/so that'). آورند is third‑person plural subjunctive/present used after تا to express the commanded action 'that they bring'. درگران here functions as an object/phrase meaning 'other/various' or 'the other (things)' in this clause.
segments.s393-g28: This clause states the thing brought: سزاوار modifies the following noun phrase as 'fitting/appropriate'. چوب گران = 'a heavy/valuable stick or staff' (چوب here = staff/club, گران = heavy/precious). آورند again is 'they brought' (third‑person plural). Word order places the modifier سزاوار before the noun phrase it qualifies.
segments.s393-g29: یکی is an indefinite 'one/ a certain'. نغز is an adjective 'fine/beautiful'. گردون here is adjectival, 'of the sky/heavenly' (used poetically as a laudative). چوبین = 'wooden/clublike'. بساخت is an archaic past tense 'he made/constructed' (ب‑ + ساخت) used in epic language.
segments.s393-g30: به گرد = 'around' (به + گرد 'about, around'); اندرش = اندر ('in/within') + the clitic ش 'his/it' meaning 'in/around it'. تیغها is the plural 'the blades/swords'. در here is the preposition 'in/into' used before the verbal action; نشاخت is an archaic past verb meaning 'he fixed/fastened/inserted' (classical epic usage). The whole phrase says he set blades around/into it.
segments.s393-g31: به سر بر is an archaic circumpositional chunk meaning 'upon the top (of)'. یکی is 'one/a certain'. گرد صندوق = 'round chest' (گرد used adjectivally = round). نغز follows as an adjective meaning 'fine/dainty' modifying the chest. The word order places location ('on the top of one round chest') before the next clause.
segments.s393-g32: بیاراست is the past tense 'he adorned/ornamented' (بیاراستن). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. درگر here functions as 'other/different' modifying what follows. پاک modifies مغز: پاک مغز = 'pure‑core' or 'pure‑hearted' (poetic compound); together the phrase means 'he adorned that other with a pure core/innermost ornament'.
segments.s393-g89: ز + noun marks origin ('from'); گنجور here is a noun 'treasury' (not an adjective), followed by the emphatic/possessive خود 'his/own'. جامه نو is a noun phrase 'a new garment/robe'. بجست is an archaic past form meaning 'took/selected' (used here as the main past verb). The word order is typical older Persian: preposition + source, possessor phrase, object phrase, then verb.
segments.s393-g90: به + آب اندر آمد is a prepositional phrase 'entered into the water' with آمد in the simple past 'came/entered'. سر و تن is a coordinated direct object 'head and body', and بشست is the past transitive 'washed (him)'. Word order: motion verb with destination, then coordinated object and verb of action.
segments.s393-g91: بیامد is a past-tense narrative verb 'came'. به پیش is a compound prepositional phrase 'into the presence of' (به + پیش), and خداوند پاک is the noun phrase 'the Pure Lord/sovereign' with پاک as an epithet. The whole segment is a straightforward past-clause describing arrival.
segments.s393-g92: همی before a past verb gives a continuous or iterative past sense ('kept/was'), so همی گشت پیچان = 'kept turning/twisting'. گشت is the past of گشتن 'to turn/become/roam' used here as 'turned/was turning'; پیچان is a participial/adverbial form 'twistingly'. و links to گریان 'weeping' (present participle used as adjective). به خاک indicates the place 'on the ground'.
segments.s393-g93: همی گفت is a conventional narrative formula ('he said' / 'he kept saying') with همی adding ongoing or emphatic aspect. کاین introduces the demonstrative 'this', and اژدها را uses the object marker را to mark 'this dragon' as definite object. که before کشت introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that killed' with کشت here meaning 'killing'.
segments.s393-g94: مگر here is an interrogative/exceptive particle used rhetorically ('unless' / 'was it anyone but...'). آن‌که is a fused relative 'the one who', and بودش is the past form of بودن with the enclitic -ش ('it was' / 'there was to him'); together they mark 'unless it was the one who was...'. جهاندار is a title 'the world-ruler/sovereign' and پشت in this context functions as 'support/protector' (a figurative 'backing' or champion). The segment thus asks for an exception: 'was it anyone other than the sovereign's protector?'
segments.s393-g95: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش 'his army'. همه خواندند is a past plural verb 'all cried/sang' (خواندند) and here governs the quoted exclamation آفرین 'Bravo!' — a conventional acclamation. The verb is plural to agree with سپاهش همه (the whole army acting).
segments.s393-g96: همه again marks 'all/everyone'; پیش دادار is a prepositional phrase 'before the Creator' (پیش = in the presence of). سر بر زمین is an idiomatic phrase 'head upon the ground' (بر is the preposition 'on/upon'), describing the action of bowing; the verb is implied from context (they laid/bowed their heads).
segments.s393-g33: به صندوق در is a locative prepositional phrase meaning “to/at the chest” (here در functions more like ‘door/inside’ in a poetic idiom). مرد دیهیم جوی is a nominal phrase: مرد (man) modified by دیهیم (rustic, villager) and جوی (seeking/searching), so together “the countryman who was searching.” The verb that completes the clause is in the next segment.
segments.s393-g34: دو اسپ گرانمایه is “two precious steeds” with the adjective following the noun (poetic order). بست is the main verb (past of بستن here: ‘he tied/fastened’). اندر اوی is a locative phrase “within it” (اندر = in/within; اوی = it/him), and the subject (he/the countryman) is supplied from the previous segment.
segments.s393-g35: نشست is a noun meaning “sitting/session.” آزمون را is the trial marked by the object/definiteness marker را, yielding “the sitting of the trial.” به صندوق شاه is a locative genitive phrase “at the king’s chest” (صندوق شاه = the king’s chest, poetic postposed possessive).
segments.s393-g36: زمانی is an adverb “for a while.” همی is an archaic habitual/continuous particle meaning roughly “kept on / was (still)”; together with the past verb راند it gives a continuous or iterative past sense (“was driving / drove for a while”). اسپان is the plural object “horses,” and به راه is the directional phrase “on/along the road.”
segments.s393-g37: زره‌دار is a compound noun meaning “armored man/warrior” (one wearing/holding armor). با introduces an accompaniment or instrument (“with”), and خنجر کابلی is “a Kabul dagger” (noun + adjective; in poetry the modifier follows).
segments.s393-g38: به سر is the locative “on (the) head.” بر نهاده is the verbal phrase “has placed/put upon” (نهاده = placed/put; بر = upon) with the verb after the locative as is common in older/poetic Persian. کلاه یلی is “a valiant/heroic cap” (کلاه = hat/helmet; یلی = valiant), modifying the object placed on the head; the subject is the armored man from the previous segment.
segments.s393-g39: چو شد is a temporal clause head “when it happened / when it came to pass.” جنگ is the noun “war/battle,” and آن اژدها ساخته is a reduced clause meaning “that dragon made/wrought” (ساخته = made/wrought) — overall: “when that dragon made war.”
segments.s393-g40: جهانجوی is an epithet ‘world‑seeker’ (an ambitious/questing person). ز این رنج is a prepositional phrase “from/away from this suffering,” and پرداخته is a past participle meaning here that the person has “paid/dealt with / been freed from” the hardship (poetic usage of پرداختن). The phrase describes the state of the world‑seeker at that time; the clause is elliptical and likely continues in the following lines.
segments.s393-g57: ز جای اندر آمد is a compact phrase: ز جای (from its/place) + اندر آمد (entered/came in). اندر is a locative preposition/adverb 'in/into' placed before the verb; آمد is the past tense 'came'. چو introduces a simile that follows the verb: چو کوه سیاه = 'like a black mountain.' The adjective سیاه follows the noun it modifies as usual.
segments.s393-g58: تو گفتی is literally the 2nd-person past 'you said' (تو = you; گفتی = you said). که introduces the reported content clause. تاریک شد is a past-tense result clause 'became dark' (تاریک = dark; شد = became), and چرخ و ماه ('the sky and the moon') follows the verb here (verb before its coordinated subjects), which is a normal word-order choice in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s393-g59: دو چشمش places the numeral + noun before the possessive clitic -ش ('his two eyes'). چو introduces the simile: چو دو چشمه تابان = 'like two shining springs.' ز خون is a prepositional phrase 'of/from blood' attached to the image (i.e. the springs are of blood). The whole segment is a descriptive nominal phrase rather than a full clause.
segments.s393-g60: همی before the verb gives a continuous/iterative coloring ('constantly' or 'was repeatedly'). آمد is the past tense 'came' used here to mean 'issued/appeared.' ز کامش برون is a loca/origin phrase 'out from his کام' — کامش (کام + possessive -ش) can mean 'his mouth' or 'his desire'; context (fire issuing) makes 'mouth' the natural reading. برون = 'out/forth'.
segments.s393-g61: چو here functions temporally 'when.' اسفندیار is the subject; آن شگفتی is the direct object/phrase 'that marvel/wonder' (آن = that; شگفتی = marvel). بدید is the past tense 'he saw.' The clause structure is standard: temporal marker + subject + object + past verb.
segments.s393-g62: به یزدان پناهید is a verb phrase 'took refuge in God' (به + object + پناهید, past-tense verb). و links the two coordinated verbs. دم درکشید is a second past action: دم = 'breath/moment' and درکشید (در + کشید) here means 'drew in' (i.e. drew in his breath or held his breath). Both verbs are third-person past actions of the same subject (Esfandiyar).
segments.s393-g63: همی jُست اسپ is 'the steed leapt' with همی marking iterative/ongoing action. اسپ is the subject placed after the verb here; جست is the past 'leapt/sprung.' از گزندش رها is a short phrase 'free from his harm/assault' (از = from; گزندش = his harm/assault; رها = free), describing the horse's being released from danger.
segments.s393-g64: به دم is an adverbial 'at the moment' (literally 'to/at the breath/instant'). درکشید is the past 'drew in/dragged in' and takes اسپ را as its object; را is the definite object marker placed on اسپ. The agent (اژدها, 'the dragon') follows the object, so the clause order is (adverbial) + verb + object-ra + subject — a permitted word order in Persian narrative.
segments.s393-g65: دهن باز کرده is a verbal phrase: دهن (noun) + باز (adjective/adverb 'open') + کرده (perfective/auxiliary 'has done' from کردن) = 'having opened its mouth' / 'opened its mouth'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by کوهی سیاه, a noun phrase where کوهی (mountain-like / 'a mountain') and سیاه ('black') form the comparison 'like a black mountain'.
segments.s393-g66: همی before a past verb (کرد) is an archaic/poetic continuous marker meaning 'kept / was continually'. کرد is the light verb 'did/made' used with the verbal-adjectival غران ('roaring') to form 'kept roaring'. بدو is the preposition+pronoun 'to him/at it', and در نگاه is a locative phrase 'in/with a look' (در='in', نگاه='gaze'), so the whole chunk reads 'kept roaring at him with its gaze'.
segments.s393-g67: فرو برد is a compound verb meaning 'drove down / swallowed / plunged (down)'. اسپان و گردون are its direct objects (hyperbolically 'the horses and the sky/heavens'). به دم is an adverbial phrase 'in a moment / at the moment' (به + دم) describing how quickly the action happened.
segments.s393-g68: به صندوق در is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the chest's door' (به + صندوق + در where در here is the noun 'door'). گشت is past of گشتن used as 'became / turned (into)'. جنگی دژم are postpositive adjectives describing that door—'warlike, grim'—so 'the chest's door became grim and warlike.'
segments.s393-g69: به کامش is a locative/goal phrase 'into his/its mouth' (به + کامش, here کامش = 'its mouth'). چو + تیغ اندر آمد is a temporal clause 'when a sword came in' (چو='when', اندر='in/within', آمد='came'). بماند (literally 'remained') is the main verb meaning 'it remained / it stopped / it held fast' after that intrusion.
segments.s393-g70: چو at the start of this clause is a simile 'as / like'. دریای خون is a genitive phrase 'a sea of blood' (دریای = 'sea of', خون = 'blood'). از دهان is the source phrase 'from the mouth', and برفشاند is a past-form verb meaning 'he spat/sprayed/spattered forth' (بر-فشاند: to scatter/spatter outward).
segments.s393-g71: نه ... توانست کردن is a negative past construction: نه (not) + توانست (was able/could) + کردن (infinitival/verbal noun 'to do') = 'could not do / could not get out'. بیرون is the directional adverb 'out', and ز کام is 'from the mouth/maw' (ز = from, کام = 'mouth' here). The phrase means 'could not get out from the maw.'
segments.s393-g72: چو here functions as 'when' or a connective 'as', introducing the referent: شمشیر بد تیغ is a noun phrase 'the sword, a terrible/bad blade' (بد qualifies تیغ). و links to کامش نیام, where کامش again refers to 'its mouth/maw' and نیام is the noun 'scabbard/sheath'—the clause lists what could not be extracted (the sword and its scabbard).
segments.s393-g17: همی is an emphatic/habitual particle meaning 'continually' or 'always'. آتش is the direct object 'fire' placed before the verb (typical SOV flexibility in classical Persian). افروزد is a 3rd-person singular present/habitual form of افروختن 'to kindle'. از کام اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from its desire' (از = 'from', کام = 'desire', اوی = archaic third-person pronoun 'he/it / its'), so the clause literally 'continually kindles fire from its desire.'
segments.s393-g18: یکی here is an emphatic/indefinite 'a certain' or 'indeed' (adds emphasis). The verbless nominal clause follows: کوه خاراست اندام اوی = 'a mountain of thorns is its body' — خاراست is poetic/contracted 'خار است' ('is thorny/consists of thorns'), اندام اوی = 'its body' (اوی = possessive 'his/its'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s393-g19: از این راه‌گر بازگردی is a conditional clause: از این راه + گر (classical conditional particle 'if') + بازگردی ('you turn back/return'). Here راه‌گر is written together but functions classically with گر as the 'if' particle; رواست = 'it is fitting/appropriate' (روا + است). The clause therefore means 'If you turn back from this way, it is fitting.'
segments.s393-g20: روانت = 'your soul/spirit' (روان + ـت possessive). بر این پند من is 'to/on this advice of mine' (بر = 'on/upon/to', این پند = 'this advice', من = 'my'). گواست (poetic form of 'گوا/گواه است') means 'is witness' — so the clause says 'your soul is witness to this advice of mine.' Note the preposition بر marks the relation 'witness to' or 'upon' the advice.
segments.s393-g21: دریغت ('your regret/pity', دریغ + ـت) is the (semantic) subject/affected item. نیاید is the negative present/optative meaning 'should not come / do not let it come' (lit. 'does not come' used idiomatically 'do not be/feel'). همی is emphatic 'indeed/always' and خویشتن is emphatic 'yourself' (emphatic reflexive). The phrase functions as an admonition: 'Let there be no regret — you yourself ...'.
segments.s393-g22: سپاهی شده is a predicative phrase: سپاهی ('army/soldiers' used collectively) + شده ('has become') = 'has become an army' or 'you have become many as an army'. ز این نشان انجمن is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/from this sign of the gathering' (ز = 'by/from', این نشان = 'this sign/token', انجمن = 'assembly/gathering'), giving the cause or means: 'by this sign of the assembly.'
segments.s393-g23: چنین داد پاسخ = 'he gave such an answer / he replied thus' (چنین = 'thus', داد = past 'gave', here 'gave an answer'). The particle که introduces reported speech ('that / saying'), and the following ای (exclamatory particle) marks the start of the quoted utterance; بدنشان = 'their bodies'. So this segment sets up the quotation: 'He answered thus: "— their bodies ..."'.
segments.s393-g24: به بندت همی برد خواهم کشان is the quoted action. به بندت = 'to/into your fetter/bond' (به = 'to/into', بندت = 'your fetter/bond'). همی is emphatic/habitual. برد here is a verbal element 'to take/lead' but in this poetic sequence it combines with خواهم کشان ('I will drag' — خواهم = auxiliary future 'I will', کشان = present participial/gerundial form 'dragging') so the whole phrase means 'I will take/lead, dragging, into your bond' — i.e. 'I will drag them into your fetters.' Note برد does not carry its ordinary nominal senses here but functions as part of a composite/poetic verbal phrase.

sentences.s393-s49: They laid (them) down and said to the Creator, "You are pure, faultless, and Lord."
sentences.s393-s41: Peshotan too came at that time, close to that renowned man of the world.
sentences.s393-s42: When the world-seeker opened his eyes, he cried out to the headstrong troop.
sentences.s393-s43: ‘I became unconscious from the poisonous smoke; nothing at all came to me from his wound for any help.’
sentences.s393-s44: He rose from that dust and went toward the water, like a man who falls unconscious into sleep.
sentences.s393-s1: He ordered that the wolf‑like man come before him; the ill‑minded and ill‑fated one came.
sentences.s393-s2: He gave three cups of ruby‑hued wine; when Ahriman grew merry from the cup.
sentences.s393-s3: He said to him, 'O wretched, despised man, what will tomorrow's fate bring?'
sentences.s393-s4: He said to him, 'O king of superior character, may evil and the evildoer be far from you.'
sentences.s393-s21: The world turned like the black face of a Zangi, and from the sign of Aries the moon displayed a crown.
sentences.s393-s22: Esfandiyar rose from his sholak (small cloak); behind him the famed host moved onward.
sentences.s393-s23: On the next day, when the world became bright, the banner of night grew dark in secret.
sentences.s393-s24: Peshotan came toward Namjuy; the son, with his brother, stood before him.
sentences.s393-s25: He clad the sovereign in mail and entrusted the army to the fortunate Peshotan.
sentences.s393-s26: He brought the heavens and the lion's chest; within it the brave king sat.
sentences.s393-s27: He bound two noble steeds to him and swiftly turned his face toward the dragon.
sentences.s393-s28: From afar he heard the dragon's cry in the heavens; he saw the war‑steed striding.
sentences.s393-s37: From the heavens and those swords a grief arose; by force it brought (this) about for a short while.
sentences.s393-s38: A brave man emerged from the chest, one sharp sword in the lion's claw.
sentences.s393-s39: With the sword he cleft his brain; smoke of his poison rose up from the earth.
sentences.s393-s40: From that biting smoke he became unconscious; he fell, and became brainless and bereft of tosh.
sentences.s393-s5: If/when you rushed into battle like fire, thus you would find a way through (your) calamities.
sentences.s393-s6: You do not know what tomorrow will bring you; spare (or grant) it to your own awakened fortune.
sentences.s393-s7: When from here you reach your lodging tomorrow, one affair awaits you — and that one is enough.
sentences.s393-s8: A certain grim dragon will come before you — that (dragon) will bring fish up out of the sea with its tail/moment.
sentences.s393-s13: See that from my grip the dragon will not find release from my sharp sword.
sentences.s393-s14: He commanded that they bring other things; they brought a fitting, heavy wooden staff.
sentences.s393-s15: He fashioned one fine, heavenly wooden club and fixed blades around it.
sentences.s393-s16: He set it upon the top of a single fine, round chest and adorned that other with a pure core.
sentences.s393-s45: He took a new garment from his treasury, went into the water, and washed his head and body.
sentences.s393-s46: He came into the presence of the Pure Lord; he kept turning and weeping on the ground.
sentences.s393-s47: He kept saying, 'Who killed this dragon? Was it anyone other than the sovereign's protector?'
sentences.s393-s48: His entire army cried 'Bravo!'; all bowed their heads to the ground before the Creator.
sentences.s393-s17: A countryman who was searching went to the chest and tied two precious steeds inside it.
sentences.s393-s18: The sitting of the trial was at the king’s chest; for a while he drove the horses along the road.
sentences.s393-s19: The armored warrior, with a Kabul dagger, had placed a valiant cap upon his head.
sentences.s393-s20: When that dragon waged war, the world‑seeker, having dealt with/been freed from this suffering, ...
sentences.s393-s29: From its place it came in like a black mountain; you said that the sky and the moon became dark.
sentences.s393-s30: His two eyes, like two shining springs of blood; constantly fire came forth from his mouth.
sentences.s393-s31: When Esfandiyar saw that marvel, he took refuge in God and drew in his breath.
sentences.s393-s32: The steed leaped free from his harm; at that moment the dragon drew in the steed.
sentences.s393-s33: It opened its mouth like a black mountain and, staring, kept roaring at him.
sentences.s393-s34: In a moment it swallowed the horses and the heavens; the chest's lid/door became grim and warlike.
sentences.s393-s35: When a sword entered its maw it stayed (there), and like a sea of blood it spouted from its mouth.
sentences.s393-s36: Nor could it be pulled out from its maw — the sword, that terrible blade, and its scabbard.
sentences.s393-s9: It constantly kindles fire from its desire; its body is a mountain of thorns.
sentences.s393-s10: If you turn back from this road, it is fitting; your soul is witness to this advice of mine.
sentences.s393-s11: Do not feel regret — you yourself have, by this sign of the assembly, become an army.
sentences.s393-s12: He answered thus: 'Their bodies — I will drag them into your bonds.'
